message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 7
Recent Green phase: Implemented the building-block rule. Components (rune) are now grouped by type and priced as floor(n/3) blocks of 60 plus singles of 25; main items stay per-item. Added helpers: isComponent, componentBasePremium, countByType, basePremium.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll refactor the implementation. Let me start by reading the current state of both files.

The implementation described in the Green phase summary (with helpers `isComponent`, `componentBasePremium`, `countByType`, `basePremium`) doesn't match what I see in the file. The current file uses a simpler `basePremiumFor` / `totalBasePremium` approach. Let me run the tests to confirm the actual state.

content.content: 38 /tmp/impl.ts

<tool_use_error>String to replace not found in file.
String: const basePremium = (items: Item[]): number => {
  const componentItems = items.filter(isComponent);
  const mainItems = items.filter((item) => !isComponent(item));

  const mainTotal = mainItems.reduce(
    (sum, item) => sum + (BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type] ?? 0),
    0
  );

  const componentCounts = countByType(componentItems);
  let componentTotal = 0;
  for (const [type, count] of componentCounts) {
    componentTotal += componentBasePremium(count, BASE_PREMIUMS[type] ?? 0);
  }

  return mainTotal + componentTotal;
};</tool_use_error>
EXIT: 1

content.text:
Important finding: The actual implementation file does NOT match the Green phase summary I was given. The file on disk is the older 7-test version with `basePremiumFor` / `totalBasePremium` and NO building-block logic. As a result:

1. The "3 runes → 65" test is currently FAILING (gets 80, since runes are priced flat at 25 each, 3×25 = 75 + 5 = 80).
2. Only 6 tests pass, not 7.
3. My edit failed because the described helpers (`isComponent`, `componentBasePremium`, `countByType`, `basePremium`) do not exist in the file.

I am the refactor agent. My contract is to refactor while keeping all tests green — I must not break tests, and I must not implement new behavior (that is the Green phase's job). But the tests are already red on disk before I touched anything. My single edit attempt failed to apply (string not found), so the file is unchanged from what I first read. Let me confirm the file is in its original state.

input.description: Confirm impl file unchanged vs original copy

content.content:
0c00ecac4ece7c05f5f871542944b070  /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_22-17-53_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.2-with-why-cleaned_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts
0c00ecac4ece7c05f5f871542944b070  /tmp/impl.ts

content.text:
The file is in its original state (hashes match) — my failed edit did not modify anything. Good.

Now I face a decision. The test suite is RED before I make any change (the "3 runes → 65" test fails). The refactor contract is "keep all tests green," but they aren't green. I cannot legitimately refactor red code into green code by adding the building-block feature — that is implementation work belonging to the Green phase, not refactoring.

However, the file as-is contains real refactoring opportunities in the code that IS present and passing (the 6 base-premium tests). I can make a behavior-preserving improvement there without touching the missing feature, and I will NOT make the red test pass (that's not my job and would mask the orchestration mismatch).

Let me evaluate the existing passing code for a safe, behavior-preserving refactoring.

**Naming Evaluation (first priority):**
- `basePremiumFor(item)` — clearly reveals intent: the base premium for one item. Good.
- `totalBasePremium(items)` — clearly reveals it sums base premiums across items. Good.
- `quote` — clearly reveals intent. Good.

Naming is already clear; no rename needed.

**APP mass of current `basePremiumFor` + `totalBasePremium`:**

`basePremiumFor`: bindings (`item`) 1; invocation (index access `BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type]` — property access ~ treat as binding) ; `??` is a conditional = 4. Roughly: bindings 2 + conditional 4 ≈ 6.

`totalBasePremium`: bindings (`items`, `sum`, `item`) 3; invocations (`reduce`, `basePremiumFor`, `+`) = 6; loop (reduce) 5; constant `0` = 1. ≈ 15.

The two functions are tiny and each reveal a distinct intent (per-item vs. aggregate). Rule 4 (fewest elements) might suggest inlining `basePremiumFor` into `totalBasePremium`, but that would hurt Rule 2 (the named per-item helper reveals intent and will be the natural extension point). Rule 2 trumps Rule 4 here.

Looking honestly: the existing passing code is already clean — clear names, no duplication, minimal mass, no unnecessary abstraction. The only "refactoring" I could apply would either (a) implement the missing feature (not refactoring, and not my phase), or (b) inline a helper that harms clarity.

I'll make one small, genuinely-improving, behavior-preserving change that survives the upcoming feature work: extract the index-with-default into a named `unitPrice` helper so the lookup-with-fallback knowledge has a single, clearly-named home (it currently appears as an inline `?? 0`, and the same lookup will be needed by component pricing later). This improves Rule 2 (reveals intent: "price per unit of a type") without breaking any test.

input.new_string:
const unitPriceFor = (type: string): number => BASE_PREMIUMS[type] ?? 0;

const basePremiumFor = (item: Item): number => unitPriceFor(item.type);

content.text:
Type-check passes cleanly.

## Refactoring Complete

**CRITICAL DISCREPANCY — please read before the next Red phase:**

The Green-phase summary I was given does NOT match the file on disk.

- **Reported:** "Implemented the building-block rule... Added helpers: `isComponent`, `componentBasePremium`, `countByType`, `basePremium`. Passing tests: 7."
- **Actual file:** None of those helpers exist. The implementation is the earlier flat-pricing version (`basePremiumFor` / `totalBasePremium` only). Runes are priced at a flat 25 each, with no block discount.
- **Actual test state (before AND after my refactor):** 6 passing, **1 failing**, 33 todo — not 7 passing.

The failing test is `quote: 3 runes → base 60 G (block applies) → premium 65 G`: it expects 65 but gets 80 (3 × 25 + 5 fee), because the building-block rule was never actually implemented in this file. The Green phase for that test appears not to have landed on disk.

**Refactoring I performed (on the passing code, behavior-preserving):**

- **Naming evaluation (first priority):** `basePremiumFor`, `totalBasePremium`, `quote` all already reveal intent clearly — no renames warranted.
- **Change applied:** Extracted the type-lookup-with-fallback into a named helper `unitPriceFor(type)`, with `basePremiumFor` now delegating to it (Rule 2 — reveals intent: "price per unit of a type"; gives the `?? 0` fallback knowledge a single named home and a natural extension point).
- **APP mass:** `basePremiumFor` dropped from ~6 (binding + property access + `??` conditional) to ~3 (one delegating invocation); the conditional moved into `unitPriceFor` (~3). Net mass roughly flat (~6 → ~6), with the primary win being clarity, not mass.

**Why I did NOT make the red test green:** Implementing the building-block rule is Green-phase work, not refactoring. As the refactor agent I keep behavior constant; turning red into green by adding a feature would mask the orchestration mismatch above.

**Verification:**
- Tests: 6 passed / 1 failed / 33 todo — identical counts before and after my change (my refactor changed no behavior).
- `tsc --noEmit`: clean (exit 0).

**Recommended next step (not for me to do):** Re-run the Green phase for `quote: 3 runes → 65` against `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_22-17-53_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.2-with-why-cleaned_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts` to actually implement the building-block rule, since the previous Green phase's changes are not present on disk.

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_22-17-53_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.2-with-why-cleaned_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
